frame_label: Misogyny Jokes: Childbearing/Rearing as Women's Responsibility
generated_comment: You know, my colleague Anjali just got promoted to Principal Engineer. She has two kids, 5 and 2. I honestly don't know how she does it. I mean, managing those late-night calls and client meetings with young ones at home, it's just superhuman. Our company should really give working mothers a separate bonus just for surviving!
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment, while seemingly admiring, is misogynistic because it frames a mother's ability to balance a demanding career with childcare as 'superhuman' and extraordinary, subtly reinforcing the belief that childcare is primarily her responsibility and therefore a significant additional burden for women in the workplace, which is not typically assumed for fathers. This aligns with the 'Praising a working mother for managing both as extraordinary, while it's invisible for fathers' manifestation.
original_frame: Workplace: Menstrual and Maternity Leave Bias
persona_text: You are Divya. Age 35. A senior software engineer in Bengaluru. You are married with one child. You are venting to your husband at home after dinner. Your skip level manager pulled you into a meeting and told you that if you plan to have another child you should think carefully about whether this is the right time to take on a high visibility project. You are telling your husband what was said to you.
